4281	double		outer_path_rows = outer_path->rows;
4282	double		inner_path_rows = inner_path->rows;
4283	double		inner_path_rows_total = workspace->inner_rows_total;
4284	List	   *hashclauses = path->path_hashclauses;
4285	Cost		startup_cost = workspace->startup_cost;
4286	Cost		run_cost = workspace->run_cost;
4287	int			numbuckets = workspace->numbuckets;
4288	int			numbatches = workspace->numbatches;
4289	Cost		cpu_per_tuple;
4290	QualCost	hash_qual_cost;
4291	QualCost	qp_qual_cost;
4292	double		hashjointuples;
4293	double		virtualbuckets;
4294	Selectivity innerbucketsize;
4295	Selectivity innermcvfreq;
4296	ListCell   *hcl;
4297
4298	/* Set the number of disabled nodes. */
4299	path->jpath.path.disabled_nodes = workspace->disabled_nodes;
4300
4301	/* Mark the path with the correct row estimate */
4302	if (path->jpath.path.param_info)
4303		path->jpath.path.rows = path->jpath.path.param_info->ppi_rows;
4304	else
4305		path->jpath.path.rows = path->jpath.path.parent->rows;
4306
4307	/* For partial paths, scale row estimate. */
4308	if (path->jpath.path.parallel_workers > 0)
4309	{
4310		double		parallel_divisor = get_parallel_divisor(&path->jpath.path);
4311
4312		path->jpath.path.rows =
4313			clamp_row_est(path->jpath.path.rows / parallel_divisor);
4314	}
4315
4316	/* mark the path with estimated # of batches */
4317	path->num_batches = numbatches;
4318
4319	/* store the total number of tuples (sum of partial row estimates) */
4320	path->inner_rows_total = inner_path_rows_total;
4321
4322	/* and compute the number of "virtual" buckets in the whole join */
4323	virtualbuckets = (double) numbuckets * (double) numbatches;
4324
4325	/*
4326	 * Determine bucketsize fraction and MCV frequency for the inner relation.
4327	 * We use the smallest bucketsize or MCV frequency estimated for any
4328	 * individual hashclause; this is undoubtedly conservative.
4329	 *
4330	 * BUT: if inner relation has been unique-ified, we can assume it's good
4331	 * for hashing.  This is important both because it's the right answer, and
4332	 * because we avoid contaminating the cache with a value that's wrong for
4333	 * non-unique-ified paths.
4334	 */
4335	if (IsA(inner_path, UniquePath))
4336	{
4337		innerbucketsize = 1.0 / virtualbuckets;
4338		innermcvfreq = 0.0;
4339	}
4340	else
4341	{
4342		List	   *otherclauses;
4343
4344		innerbucketsize = 1.0;
4345		innermcvfreq = 1.0;
4346
4347		/* At first, try to estimate bucket size using extended statistics. */
4348		otherclauses = estimate_multivariate_bucketsize(root,
4349														inner_path->parent,
4350														hashclauses,
4351														&innerbucketsize);
4352
4353		/* Pass through the remaining clauses */
4354		foreach(hcl, otherclauses)
4355		{
4356			RestrictInfo *restrictinfo = lfirst_node(RestrictInfo, hcl);
4357			Selectivity thisbucketsize;
4358			Selectivity thismcvfreq;
4359
4360			/*
4361			 * First we have to figure out which side of the hashjoin clause
4362			 * is the inner side.
4363			 *
4364			 * Since we tend to visit the same clauses over and over when
4365			 * planning a large query, we cache the bucket stats estimates in
4366			 * the RestrictInfo node to avoid repeated lookups of statistics.
4367			 */
4368			if (bms_is_subset(restrictinfo->right_relids,
4369							  inner_path->parent->relids))
4370			{
4371				/* righthand side is inner */
4372				thisbucketsize = restrictinfo->right_bucketsize;
4373				if (thisbucketsize < 0)
4374				{
4375					/* not cached yet */
4376					estimate_hash_bucket_stats(root,
4377											   get_rightop(restrictinfo->clause),
4378											   virtualbuckets,
4379											   &restrictinfo->right_mcvfreq,
4380											   &restrictinfo->right_bucketsize);
4381					thisbucketsize = restrictinfo->right_bucketsize;
4382				}
4383				thismcvfreq = restrictinfo->right_mcvfreq;
4384			}
4385			else
4386			{
4387				Assert(bms_is_subset(restrictinfo->left_relids,
4388									 inner_path->parent->relids));
4389				/* lefthand side is inner */
4390				thisbucketsize = restrictinfo->left_bucketsize;
4391				if (thisbucketsize < 0)
4392				{
4393					/* not cached yet */
4394					estimate_hash_bucket_stats(root,
4395											   get_leftop(restrictinfo->clause),
4396											   virtualbuckets,
4397											   &restrictinfo->left_mcvfreq,
4398											   &restrictinfo->left_bucketsize);
4399					thisbucketsize = restrictinfo->left_bucketsize;
4400				}
4401				thismcvfreq = restrictinfo->left_mcvfreq;
4402			}
4403
4404			if (innerbucketsize > thisbucketsize)
4405				innerbucketsize = thisbucketsize;
4406			if (innermcvfreq > thismcvfreq)
4407				innermcvfreq = thismcvfreq;
4408		}
4409	}
4410
4411	/*
4412	 * If the bucket holding the inner MCV would exceed hash_mem, we don't
4413	 * want to hash unless there is really no other alternative, so apply
4414	 * disable_cost.  (The executor normally copes with excessive memory usage
4415	 * by splitting batches, but obviously it cannot separate equal values
4416	 * that way, so it will be unable to drive the batch size below hash_mem
4417	 * when this is true.)
4418	 */
4419	if (relation_byte_size(clamp_row_est(inner_path_rows * innermcvfreq),
4420						   inner_path->pathtarget->width) > get_hash_memory_limit())
4421		startup_cost += disable_cost;
4422
4423	/*
4424	 * Compute cost of the hashquals and qpquals (other restriction clauses)
4425	 * separately.
4426	 */
4427	cost_qual_eval(&hash_qual_cost, hashclauses, root);
4428	cost_qual_eval(&qp_qual_cost, path->jpath.joinrestrictinfo, root);
4429	qp_qual_cost.startup -= hash_qual_cost.startup;
4430	qp_qual_cost.per_tuple -= hash_qual_cost.per_tuple;
4431
4432	/* CPU costs */
4433
4434	if (path->jpath.jointype == JOIN_SEMI ||
4435		path->jpath.jointype == JOIN_ANTI ||
4436		extra->inner_unique)
4437	{
4438		double		outer_matched_rows;
4439		Selectivity inner_scan_frac;
4440
4441		/*
4442		 * With a SEMI or ANTI join, or if the innerrel is known unique, the
4443		 * executor will stop after the first match.
4444		 *
4445		 * For an outer-rel row that has at least one match, we can expect the
4446		 * bucket scan to stop after a fraction 1/(match_count+1) of the
4447		 * bucket's rows, if the matches are evenly distributed.  Since they
4448		 * probably aren't quite evenly distributed, we apply a fuzz factor of
4449		 * 2.0 to that fraction.  (If we used a larger fuzz factor, we'd have
4450		 * to clamp inner_scan_frac to at most 1.0; but since match_count is
4451		 * at least 1, no such clamp is needed now.)
4452		 */
4453		outer_matched_rows = rint(outer_path_rows * extra->semifactors.outer_match_frac);
4454		inner_scan_frac = 2.0 / (extra->semifactors.match_count + 1.0);
4455
4456		startup_cost += hash_qual_cost.startup;
4457		run_cost += hash_qual_cost.per_tuple * outer_matched_rows *
4458			clamp_row_est(inner_path_rows * innerbucketsize * inner_scan_frac) * 0.5;
4459
4460		/*
4461		 * For unmatched outer-rel rows, the picture is quite a lot different.
4462		 * In the first place, there is no reason to assume that these rows
4463		 * preferentially hit heavily-populated buckets; instead assume they
4464		 * are uncorrelated with the inner distribution and so they see an
4465		 * average bucket size of inner_path_rows / virtualbuckets.  In the
4466		 * second place, it seems likely that they will have few if any exact
4467		 * hash-code matches and so very few of the tuples in the bucket will
4468		 * actually require eval of the hash quals.  We don't have any good
4469		 * way to estimate how many will, but for the moment assume that the
4470		 * effective cost per bucket entry is one-tenth what it is for
4471		 * matchable tuples.
4472		 */
4473		run_cost += hash_qual_cost.per_tuple *
4474			(outer_path_rows - outer_matched_rows) *
4475			clamp_row_est(inner_path_rows / virtualbuckets) * 0.05;
4476
4477		/* Get # of tuples that will pass the basic join */
4478		if (path->jpath.jointype == JOIN_ANTI)
4479			hashjointuples = outer_path_rows - outer_matched_rows;
4480		else
4481			hashjointuples = outer_matched_rows;
4482	}
4483	else
4484	{
4485		/*
4486		 * The number of tuple comparisons needed is the number of outer
4487		 * tuples times the typical number of tuples in a hash bucket, which
4488		 * is the inner relation size times its bucketsize fraction.  At each
4489		 * one, we need to evaluate the hashjoin quals.  But actually,
4490		 * charging the full qual eval cost at each tuple is pessimistic,
4491		 * since we don't evaluate the quals unless the hash values match
4492		 * exactly.  For lack of a better idea, halve the cost estimate to
4493		 * allow for that.
4494		 */
4495		startup_cost += hash_qual_cost.startup;
4496		run_cost += hash_qual_cost.per_tuple * outer_path_rows *
4497			clamp_row_est(inner_path_rows * innerbucketsize) * 0.5;
4498
4499		/*
4500		 * Get approx # tuples passing the hashquals.  We use
4501		 * approx_tuple_count here because we need an estimate done with
4502		 * JOIN_INNER semantics.
4503		 */
4504		hashjointuples = approx_tuple_count(root, &path->jpath, hashclauses);
4505	}
4506
4507	/*
4508	 * For each tuple that gets through the hashjoin proper, we charge
4509	 * cpu_tuple_cost plus the cost of evaluating additional restriction
4510	 * clauses that are to be applied at the join.  (This is pessimistic since
4511	 * not all of the quals may get evaluated at each tuple.)
4512	 */
4513	startup_cost += qp_qual_cost.startup;
4514	cpu_per_tuple = cpu_tuple_cost + qp_qual_cost.per_tuple;
4515	run_cost += cpu_per_tuple * hashjointuples;
4516
4517	/* tlist eval costs are paid per output row, not per tuple scanned */
4518	startup_cost += path->jpath.path.pathtarget->cost.startup;
4519	run_cost += path->jpath.path.pathtarget->cost.per_tuple * path->jpath.path.rows;
4520
4521	path->jpath.path.startup_cost = startup_cost;
4522	path->jpath.path.total_cost = startup_cost + run_cost;
4523}
4524
4525
4526/*
4527 * cost_subplan
4528 *		Figure the costs for a SubPlan (or initplan).
4529 *
4530 * Note: we could dig the subplan's Plan out of the root list, but in practice
4531 * all callers have it handy already, so we make them pass it.
4532 */
4533void
4534cost_subplan(PlannerInfo *root, SubPlan *subplan, Plan *plan)
4535{
4536	QualCost	sp_cost;
4537
4538	/*
4539	 * Figure any cost for evaluating the testexpr.
4540	 *
4541	 * Usually, SubPlan nodes are built very early, before we have constructed
4542	 * any RelOptInfos for the parent query level, which means the parent root
4543	 * does not yet contain enough information to safely consult statistics.
4544	 * Therefore, we pass root as NULL here.  cost_qual_eval() is already
4545	 * well-equipped to handle a NULL root.
4546	 *
4547	 * One exception is SubPlan nodes built for the initplans of MIN/MAX
4548	 * aggregates from indexes (cf. SS_make_initplan_from_plan).  In this
4549	 * case, having a NULL root is safe because testexpr will be NULL.
4550	 * Besides, an initplan will by definition not consult anything from the
4551	 * parent plan.
4552	 */
4553	cost_qual_eval(&sp_cost,
4554				   make_ands_implicit((Expr *) subplan->testexpr),
4555				   NULL);
4556
4557	if (subplan->useHashTable)
4558	{
4559		/*
4560		 * If we are using a hash table for the subquery outputs, then the
4561		 * cost of evaluating the query is a one-time cost.  We charge one
4562		 * cpu_operator_cost per tuple for the work of loading the hashtable,
4563		 * too.
4564		 */
4565		sp_cost.startup += plan->total_cost +
4566			cpu_operator_cost * plan->plan_rows;
4567
4568		/*
4569		 * The per-tuple costs include the cost of evaluating the lefthand
4570		 * expressions, plus the cost of probing the hashtable.  We already
4571		 * accounted for the lefthand expressions as part of the testexpr, and
4572		 * will also have counted one cpu_operator_cost for each comparison
4573		 * operator.  That is probably too low for the probing cost, but it's
4574		 * hard to make a better estimate, so live with it for now.
4575		 */
4576	}
4577	else
4578	{
4579		/*
4580		 * Otherwise we will be rescanning the subplan output on each
4581		 * evaluation.  We need to estimate how much of the output we will
4582		 * actually need to scan.  NOTE: this logic should agree with the
4583		 * tuple_fraction estimates used by make_subplan() in
4584		 * plan/subselect.c.
4585		 */
4586		Cost		plan_run_cost = plan->total_cost - plan->startup_cost;
4587
4588		if (subplan->subLinkType == EXISTS_SUBLINK)
4589		{
4590			/* we only need to fetch 1 tuple; clamp to avoid zero divide */
4591			sp_cost.per_tuple += plan_run_cost / clamp_row_est(plan->plan_rows);
4592		}
4593		else if (subplan->subLinkType == ALL_SUBLINK ||
4594				 subplan->subLinkType == ANY_SUBLINK)
4595		{
4596			/* assume we need 50% of the tuples */
4597			sp_cost.per_tuple += 0.50 * plan_run_cost;
4598			/* also charge a cpu_operator_cost per row examined */
4599			sp_cost.per_tuple += 0.50 * plan->plan_rows * cpu_operator_cost;
4600		}
4601		else
4602		{
4603			/* assume we need all tuples */
4604			sp_cost.per_tuple += plan_run_cost;
4605		}
4606
4607		/*
4608		 * Also account for subplan's startup cost. If the subplan is
4609		 * uncorrelated or undirect correlated, AND its topmost node is one
4610		 * that materializes its output, assume that we'll only need to pay
4611		 * its startup cost once; otherwise assume we pay the startup cost
4612		 * every time.
4613		 */
4614		if (subplan->parParam == NIL &&
4615			ExecMaterializesOutput(nodeTag(plan)))
4616			sp_cost.startup += plan->startup_cost;
4617		else
4618			sp_cost.per_tuple += plan->startup_cost;
4619	}
4620
4621	subplan->startup_cost = sp_cost.startup;
4622	subplan->per_call_cost = sp_cost.per_tuple;
4623}
4624
4625
4626/*
4627 * cost_rescan
4628 *		Given a finished Path, estimate the costs of rescanning it after
4629 *		having done so the first time.  For some Path types a rescan is
4630 *		cheaper than an original scan (if no parameters change), and this
4631 *		function embodies knowledge about that.  The default is to return
4632 *		the same costs stored in the Path.  (Note that the cost estimates
4633 *		actually stored in Paths are always for first scans.)
4634 *
4635 * This function is not currently intended to model effects such as rescans
4636 * being cheaper due to disk block caching; what we are concerned with is
4637 * plan types wherein the executor caches results explicitly, or doesn't
4638 * redo startup calculations, etc.
4639 */
4640static void
4641cost_rescan(PlannerInfo *root, Path *path,
4642			Cost *rescan_startup_cost,	/* output parameters */
4643			Cost *rescan_total_cost)
4644{
4645	switch (path->pathtype)
4646	{
4647		case T_FunctionScan:
4648
4649			/*
4650			 * Currently, nodeFunctionscan.c always executes the function to
4651			 * completion before returning any rows, and caches the results in
4652			 * a tuplestore.  So the function eval cost is all startup cost
4653			 * and isn't paid over again on rescans. However, all run costs
4654			 * will be paid over again.
4655			 */
4656			*rescan_startup_cost = 0;
4657			*rescan_total_cost = path->total_cost - path->startup_cost;
4658			break;
4659		case T_HashJoin:
4660
4661			/*
4662			 * If it's a single-batch join, we don't need to rebuild the hash
4663			 * table during a rescan.
4664			 */
4665			if (((HashPath *) path)->num_batches == 1)
4666			{
4667				/* Startup cost is exactly the cost of hash table building */
4668				*rescan_startup_cost = 0;
4669				*rescan_total_cost = path->total_cost - path->startup_cost;
4670			}
4671			else
4672			{
4673				/* Otherwise, no special treatment */
4674				*rescan_startup_cost = path->startup_cost;
4675				*rescan_total_cost = path->total_cost;
4676			}
4677			break;
4678		case T_CteScan:
4679		case T_WorkTableScan:
4680			{
4681				/*
4682				 * These plan types materialize their final result in a
4683				 * tuplestore or tuplesort object.  So the rescan cost is only
4684				 * cpu_tuple_cost per tuple, unless the result is large enough
4685				 * to spill to disk.
4686				 */
4687				Cost		run_cost = cpu_tuple_cost * path->rows;
4688				double		nbytes = relation_byte_size(path->rows,
4689														path->pathtarget->width);
4690				double		work_mem_bytes = work_mem * (Size) 1024;
4691
4692				if (nbytes > work_mem_bytes)
4693				{
4694					/* It will spill, so account for re-read cost */
4695					double		npages = ceil(nbytes / BLCKSZ);
4696
4697					run_cost += seq_page_cost * npages;
4698				}
4699				*rescan_startup_cost = 0;
4700				*rescan_total_cost = run_cost;
4701			}
4702			break;
4703		case T_Material:
4704		case T_Sort:
4705			{
4706				/*
4707				 * These plan types not only materialize their results, but do
4708				 * not implement qual filtering or projection.  So they are
4709				 * even cheaper to rescan than the ones above.  We charge only
4710				 * cpu_operator_cost per tuple.  (Note: keep that in sync with
4711				 * the run_cost charge in cost_sort, and also see comments in
4712				 * cost_material before you change it.)
4713				 */
4714				Cost		run_cost = cpu_operator_cost * path->rows;
4715				double		nbytes = relation_byte_size(path->rows,
4716														path->pathtarget->width);
4717				double		work_mem_bytes = work_mem * (Size) 1024;
4718
4719				if (nbytes > work_mem_bytes)
4720				{
4721					/* It will spill, so account for re-read cost */
4722					double		npages = ceil(nbytes / BLCKSZ);
4723
4724					run_cost += seq_page_cost * npages;
4725				}
4726				*rescan_startup_cost = 0;
4727				*rescan_total_cost = run_cost;
4728			}
4729			break;
4730		case T_Memoize:
4731			/* All the hard work is done by cost_memoize_rescan */
4732			cost_memoize_rescan(root, (MemoizePath *) path,
4733								rescan_startup_cost, rescan_total_cost);
4734			break;
4735		default:
4736			*rescan_startup_cost = path->startup_cost;
4737			*rescan_total_cost = path->total_cost;
4738			break;
4739	}
4740}
4741
4742
4743/*
4744 * cost_qual_eval
4745 *		Estimate the CPU costs of evaluating a WHERE clause.
4746 *		The input can be either an implicitly-ANDed list of boolean
4747 *		expressions, or a list of RestrictInfo nodes.  (The latter is
4748 *		preferred since it allows caching of the results.)
4749 *		The result includes both a one-time (startup) component,
4750 *		and a per-evaluation component.
4751 *
4752 * Note: in some code paths root can be passed as NULL, resulting in
4753 * slightly worse estimates.
4754 */
4755void
4756cost_qual_eval(QualCost *cost, List *quals, PlannerInfo *root)
4757{
4758	cost_qual_eval_context context;
4759	ListCell   *l;
4760
4761	context.root = root;
4762	context.total.startup = 0;
4763	context.total.per_tuple = 0;
4764
4765	/* We don't charge any cost for the implicit ANDing at top level ... */
4766
4767	foreach(l, quals)
4768	{
4769		Node	   *qual = (Node *) lfirst(l);
4770
4771		cost_qual_eval_walker(qual, &context);
4772	}
4773
4774	*cost = context.total;
4775}
4776
4777/*
4778 * cost_qual_eval_node
4779 *		As above, for a single RestrictInfo or expression.
4780 */
4781void
4782cost_qual_eval_node(QualCost *cost, Node *qual, PlannerInfo *root)
4783{
4784	cost_qual_eval_context context;
4785
4786	context.root = root;
4787	context.total.startup = 0;
4788	context.total.per_tuple = 0;
4789
4790	cost_qual_eval_walker(qual, &context);
4791
4792	*cost = context.total;
4793}
4794
4795static bool
4796cost_qual_eval_walker(Node *node, cost_qual_eval_context *context)
4797{
4798	if (node == NULL)
4799		return false;
4800
4801	/*
4802	 * RestrictInfo nodes contain an eval_cost field reserved for this
4803	 * routine's use, so that it's not necessary to evaluate the qual clause's
4804	 * cost more than once.  If the clause's cost hasn't been computed yet,
4805	 * the field's startup value will contain -1.
4806	 */
4807	if (IsA(node, RestrictInfo))
4808	{
4809		RestrictInfo *rinfo = (RestrictInfo *) node;
4810
4811		if (rinfo->eval_cost.startup < 0)
4812		{
4813			cost_qual_eval_context locContext;
4814
4815			locContext.root = context->root;
4816			locContext.total.startup = 0;
4817			locContext.total.per_tuple = 0;
4818
4819			/*
4820			 * For an OR clause, recurse into the marked-up tree so that we
4821			 * set the eval_cost for contained RestrictInfos too.
4822			 */
4823			if (rinfo->orclause)
4824				cost_qual_eval_walker((Node *) rinfo->orclause, &locContext);
4825			else
4826				cost_qual_eval_walker((Node *) rinfo->clause, &locContext);
4827
4828			/*
4829			 * If the RestrictInfo is marked pseudoconstant, it will be tested
4830			 * only once, so treat its cost as all startup cost.
4831			 */
4832			if (rinfo->pseudoconstant)
4833			{
4834				/* count one execution during startup */
4835				locContext.total.startup += locContext.total.per_tuple;
4836				locContext.total.per_tuple = 0;
4837			}
4838			rinfo->eval_cost = locContext.total;
4839		}
4840		context->total.startup += rinfo->eval_cost.startup;
4841		context->total.per_tuple += rinfo->eval_cost.per_tuple;
4842		/* do NOT recurse into children */
4843		return false;
4844	}
4845
4846	/*
4847	 * For each operator or function node in the given tree, we charge the
4848	 * estimated execution cost given by pg_proc.procost (remember to multiply
4849	 * this by cpu_operator_cost).
4850	 *
4851	 * Vars and Consts are charged zero, and so are boolean operators (AND,
4852	 * OR, NOT). Simplistic, but a lot better than no model at all.
4853	 *
4854	 * Should we try to account for the possibility of short-circuit
4855	 * evaluation of AND/OR?  Probably *not*, because that would make the
4856	 * results depend on the clause ordering, and we are not in any position
4857	 * to expect that the current ordering of the clauses is the one that's
4858	 * going to end up being used.  The above per-RestrictInfo caching would
4859	 * not mix well with trying to re-order clauses anyway.
4860	 *
4861	 * Another issue that is entirely ignored here is that if a set-returning
4862	 * function is below top level in the tree, the functions/operators above
4863	 * it will need to be evaluated multiple times.  In practical use, such
4864	 * cases arise so seldom as to not be worth the added complexity needed;
4865	 * moreover, since our rowcount estimates for functions tend to be pretty
4866	 * phony, the results would also be pretty phony.
4867	 */
4868	if (IsA(node, FuncExpr))
4869	{
4870		add_function_cost(context->root, ((FuncExpr *) node)->funcid, node,
4871						  &context->total);
4872	}
4873	else if (IsA(node, OpExpr) ||
4874			 IsA(node, DistinctExpr) ||
4875			 IsA(node, NullIfExpr))
4876	{
4877		/* rely on struct equivalence to treat these all alike */
4878		set_opfuncid((OpExpr *) node);
4879		add_function_cost(context->root, ((OpExpr *) node)->opfuncid, node,
4880						  &context->total);
4881	}
4882	else if (IsA(node, ScalarArrayOpExpr))
4883	{
4884		ScalarArrayOpExpr *saop = (ScalarArrayOpExpr *) node;
4885		Node	   *arraynode = (Node *) lsecond(saop->args);
4886		QualCost	sacosts;
4887		QualCost	hcosts;
4888		double		estarraylen = estimate_array_length(context->root, arraynode);
4889
4890		set_sa_opfuncid(saop);
4891		sacosts.startup = sacosts.per_tuple = 0;
4892		add_function_cost(context->root, saop->opfuncid, NULL,
4893						  &sacosts);
4894
4895		if (OidIsValid(saop->hashfuncid))
4896		{
4897			/* Handle costs for hashed ScalarArrayOpExpr */
4898			hcosts.startup = hcosts.per_tuple = 0;
4899
4900			add_function_cost(context->root, saop->hashfuncid, NULL, &hcosts);
4901			context->total.startup += sacosts.startup + hcosts.startup;
4902
4903			/* Estimate the cost of building the hashtable. */
4904			context->total.startup += estarraylen * hcosts.per_tuple;
4905
4906			/*
4907			 * XXX should we charge a little bit for sacosts.per_tuple when
4908			 * building the table, or is it ok to assume there will be zero
4909			 * hash collision?
4910			 */
4911
4912			/*
4913			 * Charge for hashtable lookups.  Charge a single hash and a
4914			 * single comparison.
4915			 */
4916			context->total.per_tuple += hcosts.per_tuple + sacosts.per_tuple;
4917		}
4918		else
4919		{
4920			/*
4921			 * Estimate that the operator will be applied to about half of the
4922			 * array elements before the answer is determined.
4923			 */
4924			context->total.startup += sacosts.startup;
4925			context->total.per_tuple += sacosts.per_tuple *
4926				estimate_array_length(context->root, arraynode) * 0.5;
4927		}
4928	}
4929	else if (IsA(node, Aggref) ||
4930			 IsA(node, WindowFunc))
4931	{
4932		/*
4933		 * Aggref and WindowFunc nodes are (and should be) treated like Vars,
4934		 * ie, zero execution cost in the current model, because they behave
4935		 * essentially like Vars at execution.  We disregard the costs of
4936		 * their input expressions for the same reason.  The actual execution
4937		 * costs of the aggregate/window functions and their arguments have to
4938		 * be factored into plan-node-specific costing of the Agg or WindowAgg
4939		 * plan node.
4940		 */
4941		return false;			/* don't recurse into children */
4942	}
4943	else if (IsA(node, GroupingFunc))
4944	{
4945		/* Treat this as having cost 1 */
4946		context->total.per_tuple += cpu_operator_cost;
4947		return false;			/* don't recurse into children */
4948	}
4949	else if (IsA(node, CoerceViaIO))
4950	{
4951		CoerceViaIO *iocoerce = (CoerceViaIO *) node;
4952		Oid			iofunc;
4953		Oid			typioparam;
4954		bool		typisvarlena;
4955
4956		/* check the result type's input function */
4957		getTypeInputInfo(iocoerce->resulttype,
4958						 &iofunc, &typioparam);
4959		add_function_cost(context->root, iofunc, NULL,
4960						  &context->total);
4961		/* check the input type's output function */
4962		getTypeOutputInfo(exprType((Node *) iocoerce->arg),
4963						  &iofunc, &typisvarlena);
4964		add_function_cost(context->root, iofunc, NULL,
4965						  &context->total);
4966	}
4967	else if (IsA(node, ArrayCoerceExpr))
4968	{
4969		ArrayCoerceExpr *acoerce = (ArrayCoerceExpr *) node;
4970		QualCost	perelemcost;
4971
4972		cost_qual_eval_node(&perelemcost, (Node *) acoerce->elemexpr,
4973							context->root);
4974		context->total.startup += perelemcost.startup;
4975		if (perelemcost.per_tuple > 0)
4976			context->total.per_tuple += perelemcost.per_tuple *
4977				estimate_array_length(context->root, (Node *) acoerce->arg);
4978	}
4979	else if (IsA(node, RowCompareExpr))
4980	{
4981		/* Conservatively assume we will check all the columns */
4982		RowCompareExpr *rcexpr = (RowCompareExpr *) node;
4983		ListCell   *lc;
4984
4985		foreach(lc, rcexpr->opnos)
4986		{
4987			Oid			opid = lfirst_oid(lc);
4988
4989			add_function_cost(context->root, get_opcode(opid), NULL,
4990							  &context->total);
4991		}
4992	}
4993	else if (IsA(node, MinMaxExpr) ||
4994			 IsA(node, SQLValueFunction) ||
4995			 IsA(node, XmlExpr) ||
4996			 IsA(node, CoerceToDomain) ||
4997			 IsA(node, NextValueExpr) ||
4998			 IsA(node, JsonExpr))
4999	{
5000		/* Treat all these as having cost 1 */
5001		context->total.per_tuple += cpu_operator_cost;
5002	}
5003	else if (IsA(node, SubLink))
5004	{
5005		/* This routine should not be applied to un-planned expressions */
5006		elog(ERROR, "cannot handle unplanned sub-select");
5007	}
5008	else if (IsA(node, SubPlan))
5009	{
5010		/*
5011		 * A subplan node in an expression typically indicates that the
5012		 * subplan will be executed on each evaluation, so charge accordingly.
5013		 * (Sub-selects that can be executed as InitPlans have already been
5014		 * removed from the expression.)
5015		 */
5016		SubPlan    *subplan = (SubPlan *) node;
5017
5018		context->total.startup += subplan->startup_cost;
5019		context->total.per_tuple += subplan->per_call_cost;
5020
5021		/*
5022		 * We don't want to recurse into the testexpr, because it was already
5023		 * counted in the SubPlan node's costs.  So we're done.
5024		 */
5025		return false;
5026	}
5027	else if (IsA(node, AlternativeSubPlan))
5028	{
5029		/*
5030		 * Arbitrarily use the first alternative plan for costing.  (We should
5031		 * certainly only include one alternative, and we don't yet have
5032		 * enough information to know which one the executor is most likely to
5033		 * use.)
5034		 */
5035		AlternativeSubPlan *asplan = (AlternativeSubPlan *) node;
5036
5037		return cost_qual_eval_walker((Node *) linitial(asplan->subplans),
5038									 context);
5039	}
5040	else if (IsA(node, PlaceHolderVar))
5041	{
5042		/*
5043		 * A PlaceHolderVar should be given cost zero when considering general
5044		 * expression evaluation costs.  The expense of doing the contained
5045		 * expression is charged as part of the tlist eval costs of the scan
5046		 * or join where the PHV is first computed (see set_rel_width and
5047		 * add_placeholders_to_joinrel).  If we charged it again here, we'd be
5048		 * double-counting the cost for each level of plan that the PHV
5049		 * bubbles up through.  Hence, return without recursing into the
5050		 * phexpr.
5051		 */
5052		return false;
5053	}
5054
5055	/* recurse into children */
5056	return expression_tree_walker(node, cost_qual_eval_walker, context);
5057}
5058
5059/*
5060 * get_restriction_qual_cost
5061 *	  Compute evaluation costs of a baserel's restriction quals, plus any
5062 *	  movable join quals that have been pushed down to the scan.
5063 *	  Results are returned into *qpqual_cost.
5064 *
5065 * This is a convenience subroutine that works for seqscans and other cases
5066 * where all the given quals will be evaluated the hard way.  It's not useful
5067 * for cost_index(), for example, where the index machinery takes care of
5068 * some of the quals.  We assume baserestrictcost was previously set by
5069 * set_baserel_size_estimates().
5070 */
5071static void
5072get_restriction_qual_cost(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *baserel,
5073						  ParamPathInfo *param_info,
5074						  QualCost *qpqual_cost)
5075{
5076	if (param_info)
5077	{
5078		/* Include costs of pushed-down clauses */
5079		cost_qual_eval(qpqual_cost, param_info->ppi_clauses, root);
5080
5081		qpqual_cost->startup += baserel->baserestrictcost.startup;
5082		qpqual_cost->per_tuple += baserel->baserestrictcost.per_tuple;
5083	}
5084	else
5085		*qpqual_cost = baserel->baserestrictcost;
5086}
5087
5088
5089/*
5090 * compute_semi_anti_join_factors
5091 *	  Estimate how much of the inner input a SEMI, ANTI, or inner_unique join
5092 *	  can be expected to scan.
5093 *
5094 * In a hash or nestloop SEMI/ANTI join, the executor will stop scanning
5095 * inner rows as soon as it finds a match to the current outer row.
5096 * The same happens if we have detected the inner rel is unique.
5097 * We should therefore adjust some of the cost components for this effect.
5098 * This function computes some estimates needed for these adjustments.
5099 * These estimates will be the same regardless of the particular paths used
5100 * for the outer and inner relation, so we compute these once and then pass
5101 * them to all the join cost estimation functions.
5102 *
5103 * Input parameters:
5104 *	joinrel: join relation under consideration
5105 *	outerrel: outer relation under consideration
5106 *	innerrel: inner relation under consideration
5107 *	jointype: if not JOIN_SEMI or JOIN_ANTI, we assume it's inner_unique
5108 *	sjinfo: SpecialJoinInfo relevant to this join
5109 *	restrictlist: join quals
5110 * Output parameters:
5111 *	*semifactors is filled in (see pathnodes.h for field definitions)
5112 */
5113void
5114compute_semi_anti_join_factors(PlannerInfo *root,
5115							   RelOptInfo *joinrel,
5116							   RelOptInfo *outerrel,
5117							   RelOptInfo *innerrel,
5118							   JoinType jointype,
5119							   SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo,
5120							   List *restrictlist,
5121							   SemiAntiJoinFactors *semifactors)
5122{
5123	Selectivity jselec;
5124	Selectivity nselec;
5125	Selectivity avgmatch;
5126	SpecialJoinInfo norm_sjinfo;
5127	List	   *joinquals;
5128	ListCell   *l;
5129
5130	/*
5131	 * In an ANTI join, we must ignore clauses that are "pushed down", since
5132	 * those won't affect the match logic.  In a SEMI join, we do not
5133	 * distinguish joinquals from "pushed down" quals, so just use the whole
5134	 * restrictinfo list.  For other outer join types, we should consider only
5135	 * non-pushed-down quals, so that this devolves to an IS_OUTER_JOIN check.
5136	 */
5137	if (IS_OUTER_JOIN(jointype))
5138	{
5139		joinquals = NIL;
5140		foreach(l, restrictlist)
5141		{
5142			RestrictInfo *rinfo = lfirst_node(RestrictInfo, l);
5143
5144			if (!RINFO_IS_PUSHED_DOWN(rinfo, joinrel->relids))
5145				joinquals = lappend(joinquals, rinfo);
5146		}
5147	}
5148	else
5149		joinquals = restrictlist;
5150
5151	/*
5152	 * Get the JOIN_SEMI or JOIN_ANTI selectivity of the join clauses.
5153	 */
5154	jselec = clauselist_selectivity(root,
5155									joinquals,
5156									0,
5157									(jointype == JOIN_ANTI) ? JOIN_ANTI : JOIN_SEMI,
5158									sjinfo);
5159
5160	/*
5161	 * Also get the normal inner-join selectivity of the join clauses.
5162	 */
5163	init_dummy_sjinfo(&norm_sjinfo, outerrel->relids, innerrel->relids);
5164
5165	nselec = clauselist_selectivity(root,
5166									joinquals,
5167									0,
5168									JOIN_INNER,
5169									&norm_sjinfo);
5170
5171	/* Avoid leaking a lot of ListCells */
5172	if (IS_OUTER_JOIN(jointype))
5173		list_free(joinquals);
5174
5175	/*
5176	 * jselec can be interpreted as the fraction of outer-rel rows that have
5177	 * any matches (this is true for both SEMI and ANTI cases).  And nselec is
5178	 * the fraction of the Cartesian product that matches.  So, the average
5179	 * number of matches for each outer-rel row that has at least one match is
5180	 * nselec * inner_rows / jselec.
5181	 *
5182	 * Note: it is correct to use the inner rel's "rows" count here, even
5183	 * though we might later be considering a parameterized inner path with
5184	 * fewer rows.  This is because we have included all the join clauses in
5185	 * the selectivity estimate.
5186	 */
5187	if (jselec > 0)				/* protect against zero divide */
5188	{
5189		avgmatch = nselec * innerrel->rows / jselec;
5190		/* Clamp to sane range */
5191		avgmatch = Max(1.0, avgmatch);
5192	}
5193	else
5194		avgmatch = 1.0;
5195
5196	semifactors->outer_match_frac = jselec;
5197	semifactors->match_count = avgmatch;
5198}
5199
5200/*
5201 * has_indexed_join_quals
5202 *	  Check whether all the joinquals of a nestloop join are used as
5203 *	  inner index quals.
5204 *
5205 * If the inner path of a SEMI/ANTI join is an indexscan (including bitmap
5206 * indexscan) that uses all the joinquals as indexquals, we can assume that an
5207 * unmatched outer tuple is cheap to process, whereas otherwise it's probably
5208 * expensive.
5209 */
5210static bool
5211has_indexed_join_quals(NestPath *path)
5212{
5213	JoinPath   *joinpath = &path->jpath;
5214	Relids		joinrelids = joinpath->path.parent->relids;
5215	Path	   *innerpath = joinpath->innerjoinpath;
5216	List	   *indexclauses;
5217	bool		found_one;
5218	ListCell   *lc;
5219
5220	/* If join still has quals to evaluate, it's not fast */
5221	if (joinpath->joinrestrictinfo != NIL)
5222		return false;
5223	/* Nor if the inner path isn't parameterized at all */
5224	if (innerpath->param_info == NULL)
5225		return false;
5226
5227	/* Find the indexclauses list for the inner scan */
5228	switch (innerpath->pathtype)
5229	{
5230		case T_IndexScan:
5231		case T_IndexOnlyScan:
5232			indexclauses = ((IndexPath *) innerpath)->indexclauses;
5233			break;
5234		case T_BitmapHeapScan:
5235			{
5236				/* Accept only a simple bitmap scan, not AND/OR cases */
5237				Path	   *bmqual = ((BitmapHeapPath *) innerpath)->bitmapqual;
5238
5239				if (IsA(bmqual, IndexPath))
5240					indexclauses = ((IndexPath *) bmqual)->indexclauses;
5241				else
5242					return false;
5243				break;
5244			}
5245		default:
5246
5247			/*
5248			 * If it's not a simple indexscan, it probably doesn't run quickly
5249			 * for zero rows out, even if it's a parameterized path using all
5250			 * the joinquals.
5251			 */
5252			return false;
5253	}
5254
5255	/*
5256	 * Examine the inner path's param clauses.  Any that are from the outer
5257	 * path must be found in the indexclauses list, either exactly or in an
5258	 * equivalent form generated by equivclass.c.  Also, we must find at least
5259	 * one such clause, else it's a clauseless join which isn't fast.
5260	 */
5261	found_one = false;
5262	foreach(lc, innerpath->param_info->ppi_clauses)
5263	{
5264		RestrictInfo *rinfo = (RestrictInfo *) lfirst(lc);
5265
5266		if (join_clause_is_movable_into(rinfo,
5267										innerpath->parent->relids,
5268										joinrelids))
5269		{
5270			if (!is_redundant_with_indexclauses(rinfo, indexclauses))
5271				return false;
5272			found_one = true;
5273		}
5274	}
5275	return found_one;
5276}
5277
5278
5279/*
5280 * approx_tuple_count
5281 *		Quick-and-dirty estimation of the number of join rows passing
5282 *		a set of qual conditions.
5283 *
5284 * The quals can be either an implicitly-ANDed list of boolean expressions,
5285 * or a list of RestrictInfo nodes (typically the latter).
5286 *
5287 * We intentionally compute the selectivity under JOIN_INNER rules, even
5288 * if it's some type of outer join.  This is appropriate because we are
5289 * trying to figure out how many tuples pass the initial merge or hash
5290 * join step.
5291 *
5292 * This is quick-and-dirty because we bypass clauselist_selectivity, and
5293 * simply multiply the independent clause selectivities together.  Now
5294 * clauselist_selectivity often can't do any better than that anyhow, but
5295 * for some situations (such as range constraints) it is smarter.  However,
5296 * we can't effectively cache the results of clauselist_selectivity, whereas
5297 * the individual clause selectivities can be and are cached.
5298 *
5299 * Since we are only using the results to estimate how many potential
5300 * output tuples are generated and passed through qpqual checking, it
5301 * seems OK to live with the approximation.
5302 */
5303static double
5304approx_tuple_count(PlannerInfo *root, JoinPath *path, List *quals)
5305{
5306	double		tuples;
5307	double		outer_tuples = path->outerjoinpath->rows;
5308	double		inner_tuples = path->innerjoinpath->rows;
5309	SpecialJoinInfo sjinfo;
5310	Selectivity selec = 1.0;
5311	ListCell   *l;
5312
5313	/*
5314	 * Make up a SpecialJoinInfo for JOIN_INNER semantics.
5315	 */
5316	init_dummy_sjinfo(&sjinfo, path->outerjoinpath->parent->relids,
5317					  path->innerjoinpath->parent->relids);
5318
5319	/* Get the approximate selectivity */
5320	foreach(l, quals)
5321	{
5322		Node	   *qual = (Node *) lfirst(l);
5323
5324		/* Note that clause_selectivity will be able to cache its result */
5325		selec *= clause_selectivity(root, qual, 0, JOIN_INNER, &sjinfo);
5326	}
5327
5328	/* Apply it to the input relation sizes */
5329	tuples = selec * outer_tuples * inner_tuples;
5330
5331	return clamp_row_est(tuples);
5332}
5333
5334
5335/*
5336 * set_baserel_size_estimates
5337 *		Set the size estimates for the given base relation.
5338 *
5339 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
5340 * already, and rel->tuples must be set.
5341 *
5342 * We set the following fields of the rel node:
5343 *	rows: the estimated number of output tuples (after applying
5344 *		  restriction clauses).
5345 *	width: the estimated average output tuple width in bytes.
5346 *	baserestrictcost: estimated cost of evaluating baserestrictinfo clauses.
5347 */
5348void
5349set_baserel_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
5350{
5351	double		nrows;
5352
5353	/* Should only be applied to base relations */
5354	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
5355
5356	nrows = rel->tuples *
5357		clauselist_selectivity(root,
5358							   rel->baserestrictinfo,
5359							   0,
5360							   JOIN_INNER,
5361							   NULL);
5362
5363	rel->rows = clamp_row_est(nrows);
5364
5365	cost_qual_eval(&rel->baserestrictcost, rel->baserestrictinfo, root);
5366
5367	set_rel_width(root, rel);
5368}
5369
5370/*
5371 * get_parameterized_baserel_size
5372 *		Make a size estimate for a parameterized scan of a base relation.
5373 *
5374 * 'param_clauses' lists the additional join clauses to be used.
5375 *
5376 * set_baserel_size_estimates must have been applied already.
5377 */
5378double
5379get_parameterized_baserel_size(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel,
5380							   List *param_clauses)
5381{
5382	List	   *allclauses;
5383	double		nrows;
5384
5385	/*
5386	 * Estimate the number of rows returned by the parameterized scan, knowing
5387	 * that it will apply all the extra join clauses as well as the rel's own
5388	 * restriction clauses.  Note that we force the clauses to be treated as
5389	 * non-join clauses during selectivity estimation.
5390	 */
5391	allclauses = list_concat_copy(param_clauses, rel->baserestrictinfo);
5392	nrows = rel->tuples *
5393		clauselist_selectivity(root,
5394							   allclauses,
5395							   rel->relid,	/* do not use 0! */
5396							   JOIN_INNER,
5397							   NULL);
5398	nrows = clamp_row_est(nrows);
5399	/* For safety, make sure result is not more than the base estimate */
5400	if (nrows > rel->rows)
5401		nrows = rel->rows;
5402	return nrows;
5403}
5404
5405/*
5406 * set_joinrel_size_estimates
5407 *		Set the size estimates for the given join relation.
5408 *
5409 * The rel's targetlist must have been constructed already, and a
5410 * restriction clause list that matches the given component rels must
5411 * be provided.
5412 *
5413 * Since there is more than one way to make a joinrel for more than two
5414 * base relations, the results we get here could depend on which component
5415 * rel pair is provided.  In theory we should get the same answers no matter
5416 * which pair is provided; in practice, since the selectivity estimation
5417 * routines don't handle all cases equally well, we might not.  But there's
5418 * not much to be done about it.  (Would it make sense to repeat the
5419 * calculations for each pair of input rels that's encountered, and somehow
5420 * average the results?  Probably way more trouble than it's worth, and
5421 * anyway we must keep the rowcount estimate the same for all paths for the
5422 * joinrel.)
5423 *
5424 * We set only the rows field here.  The reltarget field was already set by
5425 * build_joinrel_tlist, and baserestrictcost is not used for join rels.
5426 */
5427void
5428set_joinrel_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel,
5429						   RelOptInfo *outer_rel,
5430						   RelOptInfo *inner_rel,
5431						   SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo,
5432						   List *restrictlist)
5433{
5434	rel->rows = calc_joinrel_size_estimate(root,
5435										   rel,
5436										   outer_rel,
5437										   inner_rel,
5438										   outer_rel->rows,
5439										   inner_rel->rows,
5440										   sjinfo,
5441										   restrictlist);
5442}
5443
5444/*
5445 * get_parameterized_joinrel_size
5446 *		Make a size estimate for a parameterized scan of a join relation.
5447 *
5448 * 'rel' is the joinrel under consideration.
5449 * 'outer_path', 'inner_path' are (probably also parameterized) Paths that
5450 *		produce the relations being joined.
5451 * 'sjinfo' is any SpecialJoinInfo relevant to this join.
5452 * 'restrict_clauses' lists the join clauses that need to be applied at the
5453 * join node (including any movable clauses that were moved down to this join,
5454 * and not including any movable clauses that were pushed down into the
5455 * child paths).
5456 *
5457 * set_joinrel_size_estimates must have been applied already.
5458 */
5459double
5460get_parameterized_joinrel_size(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel,
5461							   Path *outer_path,
5462							   Path *inner_path,
5463							   SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo,
5464							   List *restrict_clauses)
5465{
5466	double		nrows;
5467
5468	/*
5469	 * Estimate the number of rows returned by the parameterized join as the
5470	 * sizes of the input paths times the selectivity of the clauses that have
5471	 * ended up at this join node.
5472	 *
5473	 * As with set_joinrel_size_estimates, the rowcount estimate could depend
5474	 * on the pair of input paths provided, though ideally we'd get the same
5475	 * estimate for any pair with the same parameterization.
5476	 */
5477	nrows = calc_joinrel_size_estimate(root,
5478									   rel,
5479									   outer_path->parent,
5480									   inner_path->parent,
5481									   outer_path->rows,
5482									   inner_path->rows,
5483									   sjinfo,
5484									   restrict_clauses);
5485	/* For safety, make sure result is not more than the base estimate */
5486	if (nrows > rel->rows)
5487		nrows = rel->rows;
5488	return nrows;
5489}
5490
5491/*
5492 * calc_joinrel_size_estimate
5493 *		Workhorse for set_joinrel_size_estimates and
5494 *		get_parameterized_joinrel_size.
5495 *
5496 * outer_rel/inner_rel are the relations being joined, but they should be
5497 * assumed to have sizes outer_rows/inner_rows; those numbers might be less
5498 * than what rel->rows says, when we are considering parameterized paths.
5499 */
5500static double
5501calc_joinrel_size_estimate(PlannerInfo *root,
5502						   RelOptInfo *joinrel,
5503						   RelOptInfo *outer_rel,
5504						   RelOptInfo *inner_rel,
5505						   double outer_rows,
5506						   double inner_rows,
5507						   SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo,
5508						   List *restrictlist)
5509{
5510	JoinType	jointype = sjinfo->jointype;
5511	Selectivity fkselec;
5512	Selectivity jselec;
5513	Selectivity pselec;
5514	double		nrows;
5515
5516	/*
5517	 * Compute joinclause selectivity.  Note that we are only considering
5518	 * clauses that become restriction clauses at this join level; we are not
5519	 * double-counting them because they were not considered in estimating the
5520	 * sizes of the component rels.
5521	 *
5522	 * First, see whether any of the joinclauses can be matched to known FK
5523	 * constraints.  If so, drop those clauses from the restrictlist, and
5524	 * instead estimate their selectivity using FK semantics.  (We do this
5525	 * without regard to whether said clauses are local or "pushed down".
5526	 * Probably, an FK-matching clause could never be seen as pushed down at
5527	 * an outer join, since it would be strict and hence would be grounds for
5528	 * join strength reduction.)  fkselec gets the net selectivity for
5529	 * FK-matching clauses, or 1.0 if there are none.
5530	 */
5531	fkselec = get_foreign_key_join_selectivity(root,
5532											   outer_rel->relids,
5533											   inner_rel->relids,
5534											   sjinfo,
5535											   &restrictlist);
5536
5537	/*
5538	 * For an outer join, we have to distinguish the selectivity of the join's
5539	 * own clauses (JOIN/ON conditions) from any clauses that were "pushed
5540	 * down".  For inner joins we just count them all as joinclauses.
5541	 */
5542	if (IS_OUTER_JOIN(jointype))
5543	{
5544		List	   *joinquals = NIL;
5545		List	   *pushedquals = NIL;
5546		ListCell   *l;
5547
5548		/* Grovel through the clauses to separate into two lists */
5549		foreach(l, restrictlist)
5550		{
5551			RestrictInfo *rinfo = lfirst_node(RestrictInfo, l);
5552
5553			if (RINFO_IS_PUSHED_DOWN(rinfo, joinrel->relids))
5554				pushedquals = lappend(pushedquals, rinfo);
5555			else
5556				joinquals = lappend(joinquals, rinfo);
5557		}
5558
5559		/* Get the separate selectivities */
5560		jselec = clauselist_selectivity(root,
5561										joinquals,
5562										0,
5563										jointype,
5564										sjinfo);
5565		pselec = clauselist_selectivity(root,
5566										pushedquals,
5567										0,
5568										jointype,
5569										sjinfo);
5570
5571		/* Avoid leaking a lot of ListCells */
5572		list_free(joinquals);
5573		list_free(pushedquals);
5574	}
5575	else
5576	{
5577		jselec = clauselist_selectivity(root,
5578										restrictlist,
5579										0,
5580										jointype,
5581										sjinfo);
5582		pselec = 0.0;			/* not used, keep compiler quiet */
5583	}
5584
5585	/*
5586	 * Basically, we multiply size of Cartesian product by selectivity.
5587	 *
5588	 * If we are doing an outer join, take that into account: the joinqual
5589	 * selectivity has to be clamped using the knowledge that the output must
5590	 * be at least as large as the non-nullable input.  However, any
5591	 * pushed-down quals are applied after the outer join, so their
5592	 * selectivity applies fully.
5593	 *
5594	 * For JOIN_SEMI and JOIN_ANTI, the selectivity is defined as the fraction
5595	 * of LHS rows that have matches, and we apply that straightforwardly.
5596	 */
5597	switch (jointype)
5598	{
5599		case JOIN_INNER:
5600			nrows = outer_rows * inner_rows * fkselec * jselec;
5601			/* pselec not used */
5602			break;
5603		case JOIN_LEFT:
5604			nrows = outer_rows * inner_rows * fkselec * jselec;
5605			if (nrows < outer_rows)
5606				nrows = outer_rows;
5607			nrows *= pselec;
5608			break;
5609		case JOIN_FULL:
5610			nrows = outer_rows * inner_rows * fkselec * jselec;
5611			if (nrows < outer_rows)
5612				nrows = outer_rows;
5613			if (nrows < inner_rows)
5614				nrows = inner_rows;
5615			nrows *= pselec;
5616			break;
5617		case JOIN_SEMI:
5618			nrows = outer_rows * fkselec * jselec;
5619			/* pselec not used */
5620			break;
5621		case JOIN_ANTI:
5622			nrows = outer_rows * (1.0 - fkselec * jselec);
5623			nrows *= pselec;
5624			break;
5625		default:
5626			/* other values not expected here */
5627			elog(ERROR, "unrecognized join type: %d", (int) jointype);
5628			nrows = 0;			/* keep compiler quiet */
5629			break;
5630	}
5631
5632	return clamp_row_est(nrows);
5633}
5634
5635/*
5636 * get_foreign_key_join_selectivity
5637 *		Estimate join selectivity for foreign-key-related clauses.
5638 *
5639 * Remove any clauses that can be matched to FK constraints from *restrictlist,
5640 * and return a substitute estimate of their selectivity.  1.0 is returned
5641 * when there are no such clauses.
5642 *
5643 * The reason for treating such clauses specially is that we can get better
5644 * estimates this way than by relying on clauselist_selectivity(), especially
5645 * for multi-column FKs where that function's assumption that the clauses are
5646 * independent falls down badly.  But even with single-column FKs, we may be
5647 * able to get a better answer when the pg_statistic stats are missing or out
5648 * of date.
5649 */
5650static Selectivity
5651get_foreign_key_join_selectivity(PlannerInfo *root,
5652								 Relids outer_relids,
5653								 Relids inner_relids,
5654								 SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo,
5655								 List **restrictlist)
5656{
5657	Selectivity fkselec = 1.0;
5658	JoinType	jointype = sjinfo->jointype;
5659	List	   *worklist = *restrictlist;
5660	ListCell   *lc;
5661
5662	/* Consider each FK constraint that is known to match the query */
5663	foreach(lc, root->fkey_list)
5664	{
5665		ForeignKeyOptInfo *fkinfo = (ForeignKeyOptInfo *) lfirst(lc);
5666		bool		ref_is_outer;
5667		List	   *removedlist;
5668		ListCell   *cell;
5669
5670		/*
5671		 * This FK is not relevant unless it connects a baserel on one side of
5672		 * this join to a baserel on the other side.
5673		 */
5674		if (bms_is_member(fkinfo->con_relid, outer_relids) &&
5675			bms_is_member(fkinfo->ref_relid, inner_relids))
5676			ref_is_outer = false;
5677		else if (bms_is_member(fkinfo->ref_relid, outer_relids) &&
5678				 bms_is_member(fkinfo->con_relid, inner_relids))
5679			ref_is_outer = true;
5680		else
5681			continue;
5682
5683		/*
5684		 * If we're dealing with a semi/anti join, and the FK's referenced
5685		 * relation is on the outside, then knowledge of the FK doesn't help
5686		 * us figure out what we need to know (which is the fraction of outer
5687		 * rows that have matches).  On the other hand, if the referenced rel
5688		 * is on the inside, then all outer rows must have matches in the
5689		 * referenced table (ignoring nulls).  But any restriction or join
5690		 * clauses that filter that table will reduce the fraction of matches.
5691		 * We can account for restriction clauses, but it's too hard to guess
5692		 * how many table rows would get through a join that's inside the RHS.
5693		 * Hence, if either case applies, punt and ignore the FK.
5694		 */
5695		if ((jointype == JOIN_SEMI || jointype == JOIN_ANTI) &&
5696			(ref_is_outer || bms_membership(inner_relids) != BMS_SINGLETON))
5697			continue;
5698
5699		/*
5700		 * Modify the restrictlist by removing clauses that match the FK (and
5701		 * putting them into removedlist instead).  It seems unsafe to modify
5702		 * the originally-passed List structure, so we make a shallow copy the
5703		 * first time through.
5704		 */
5705		if (worklist == *restrictlist)
5706			worklist = list_copy(worklist);
5707
5708		removedlist = NIL;
5709		foreach(cell, worklist)
5710		{
5711			RestrictInfo *rinfo = (RestrictInfo *) lfirst(cell);
5712			bool		remove_it = false;
5713			int			i;
5714
5715			/* Drop this clause if it matches any column of the FK */
5716			for (i = 0; i < fkinfo->nkeys; i++)
5717			{
5718				if (rinfo->parent_ec)
5719				{
5720					/*
5721					 * EC-derived clauses can only match by EC.  It is okay to
5722					 * consider any clause derived from the same EC as
5723					 * matching the FK: even if equivclass.c chose to generate
5724					 * a clause equating some other pair of Vars, it could
5725					 * have generated one equating the FK's Vars.  So for
5726					 * purposes of estimation, we can act as though it did so.
5727					 *
5728					 * Note: checking parent_ec is a bit of a cheat because
5729					 * there are EC-derived clauses that don't have parent_ec
5730					 * set; but such clauses must compare expressions that
5731					 * aren't just Vars, so they cannot match the FK anyway.
5732					 */
5733					if (fkinfo->eclass[i] == rinfo->parent_ec)
5734					{
5735						remove_it = true;
5736						break;
5737					}
5738				}
5739				else
5740				{
5741					/*
5742					 * Otherwise, see if rinfo was previously matched to FK as
5743					 * a "loose" clause.
5744					 */
5745					if (list_member_ptr(fkinfo->rinfos[i], rinfo))
5746					{
5747						remove_it = true;
5748						break;
5749					}
5750				}
5751			}
5752			if (remove_it)
5753			{
5754				worklist = foreach_delete_current(worklist, cell);
5755				removedlist = lappend(removedlist, rinfo);
5756			}
5757		}
5758
5759		/*
5760		 * If we failed to remove all the matching clauses we expected to
5761		 * find, chicken out and ignore this FK; applying its selectivity
5762		 * might result in double-counting.  Put any clauses we did manage to
5763		 * remove back into the worklist.
5764		 *
5765		 * Since the matching clauses are known not outerjoin-delayed, they
5766		 * would normally have appeared in the initial joinclause list.  If we
5767		 * didn't find them, there are two possibilities:
5768		 *
5769		 * 1. If the FK match is based on an EC that is ec_has_const, it won't
5770		 * have generated any join clauses at all.  We discount such ECs while
5771		 * checking to see if we have "all" the clauses.  (Below, we'll adjust
5772		 * the selectivity estimate for this case.)
5773		 *
5774		 * 2. The clauses were matched to some other FK in a previous
5775		 * iteration of this loop, and thus removed from worklist.  (A likely
5776		 * case is that two FKs are matched to the same EC; there will be only
5777		 * one EC-derived clause in the initial list, so the first FK will
5778		 * consume it.)  Applying both FKs' selectivity independently risks
5779		 * underestimating the join size; in particular, this would undo one
5780		 * of the main things that ECs were invented for, namely to avoid
5781		 * double-counting the selectivity of redundant equality conditions.
5782		 * Later we might think of a reasonable way to combine the estimates,
5783		 * but for now, just punt, since this is a fairly uncommon situation.
5784		 */
5785		if (removedlist == NIL ||
5786			list_length(removedlist) !=
5787			(fkinfo->nmatched_ec - fkinfo->nconst_ec + fkinfo->nmatched_ri))
5788		{
5789			worklist = list_concat(worklist, removedlist);
5790			continue;
5791		}
5792
5793		/*
5794		 * Finally we get to the payoff: estimate selectivity using the
5795		 * knowledge that each referencing row will match exactly one row in
5796		 * the referenced table.
5797		 *
5798		 * XXX that's not true in the presence of nulls in the referencing
5799		 * column(s), so in principle we should derate the estimate for those.
5800		 * However (1) if there are any strict restriction clauses for the
5801		 * referencing column(s) elsewhere in the query, derating here would
5802		 * be double-counting the null fraction, and (2) it's not very clear
5803		 * how to combine null fractions for multiple referencing columns. So
5804		 * we do nothing for now about correcting for nulls.
5805		 *
5806		 * XXX another point here is that if either side of an FK constraint
5807		 * is an inheritance parent, we estimate as though the constraint
5808		 * covers all its children as well.  This is not an unreasonable
5809		 * assumption for a referencing table, ie the user probably applied
5810		 * identical constraints to all child tables (though perhaps we ought
5811		 * to check that).  But it's not possible to have done that for a
5812		 * referenced table.  Fortunately, precisely because that doesn't
5813		 * work, it is uncommon in practice to have an FK referencing a parent
5814		 * table.  So, at least for now, disregard inheritance here.
5815		 */
5816		if (jointype == JOIN_SEMI || jointype == JOIN_ANTI)
5817		{
5818			/*
5819			 * For JOIN_SEMI and JOIN_ANTI, we only get here when the FK's
5820			 * referenced table is exactly the inside of the join.  The join
5821			 * selectivity is defined as the fraction of LHS rows that have
5822			 * matches.  The FK implies that every LHS row has a match *in the
5823			 * referenced table*; but any restriction clauses on it will
5824			 * reduce the number of matches.  Hence we take the join
5825			 * selectivity as equal to the selectivity of the table's
5826			 * restriction clauses, which is rows / tuples; but we must guard
5827			 * against tuples == 0.
5828			 */
5829			RelOptInfo *ref_rel = find_base_rel(root, fkinfo->ref_relid);
5830			double		ref_tuples = Max(ref_rel->tuples, 1.0);
5831
5832			fkselec *= ref_rel->rows / ref_tuples;
5833		}
5834		else
5835		{
5836			/*
5837			 * Otherwise, selectivity is exactly 1/referenced-table-size; but
5838			 * guard against tuples == 0.  Note we should use the raw table
5839			 * tuple count, not any estimate of its filtered or joined size.
5840			 */
5841			RelOptInfo *ref_rel = find_base_rel(root, fkinfo->ref_relid);
5842			double		ref_tuples = Max(ref_rel->tuples, 1.0);
5843
5844			fkselec *= 1.0 / ref_tuples;
5845		}
5846
5847		/*
5848		 * If any of the FK columns participated in ec_has_const ECs, then
5849		 * equivclass.c will have generated "var = const" restrictions for
5850		 * each side of the join, thus reducing the sizes of both input
5851		 * relations.  Taking the fkselec at face value would amount to
5852		 * double-counting the selectivity of the constant restriction for the
5853		 * referencing Var.  Hence, look for the restriction clause(s) that
5854		 * were applied to the referencing Var(s), and divide out their
5855		 * selectivity to correct for this.
5856		 */
5857		if (fkinfo->nconst_ec > 0)
5858		{
5859			for (int i = 0; i < fkinfo->nkeys; i++)
5860			{
5861				EquivalenceClass *ec = fkinfo->eclass[i];
5862
5863				if (ec && ec->ec_has_const)
5864				{
5865					EquivalenceMember *em = fkinfo->fk_eclass_member[i];
5866					RestrictInfo *rinfo = find_derived_clause_for_ec_member(root,
5867																			ec,
5868																			em);
5869
5870					if (rinfo)
5871					{
5872						Selectivity s0;
5873
5874						s0 = clause_selectivity(root,
5875												(Node *) rinfo,
5876												0,
5877												jointype,
5878												sjinfo);
5879						if (s0 > 0)
5880							fkselec /= s0;
5881					}
5882				}
5883			}
5884		}
5885	}
5886
5887	*restrictlist = worklist;
5888	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(fkselec);
5889	return fkselec;
5890}
5891
5892/*
5893 * set_subquery_size_estimates
5894 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a subquery.
5895 *
5896 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
5897 * already, and the Paths for the subquery must have been completed.
5898 * We look at the subquery's PlannerInfo to extract data.
5899 *
5900 * We set the same fields as set_baserel_size_estimates.
5901 */
5902void
5903set_subquery_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
5904{
5905	PlannerInfo *subroot = rel->subroot;
5906	RelOptInfo *sub_final_rel;
5907	ListCell   *lc;
5908
5909	/* Should only be applied to base relations that are subqueries */
5910	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
5911	Assert(planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root)->rtekind == RTE_SUBQUERY);
5912
5913	/*
5914	 * Copy raw number of output rows from subquery.  All of its paths should
5915	 * have the same output rowcount, so just look at cheapest-total.
5916	 */
5917	sub_final_rel = fetch_upper_rel(subroot, UPPERREL_FINAL, NULL);
5918	rel->tuples = sub_final_rel->cheapest_total_path->rows;
5919
5920	/*
5921	 * Compute per-output-column width estimates by examining the subquery's
5922	 * targetlist.  For any output that is a plain Var, get the width estimate
5923	 * that was made while planning the subquery.  Otherwise, we leave it to
5924	 * set_rel_width to fill in a datatype-based default estimate.
5925	 */
5926	foreach(lc, subroot->parse->targetList)
5927	{
5928		TargetEntry *te = lfirst_node(TargetEntry, lc);
5929		Node	   *texpr = (Node *) te->expr;
5930		int32		item_width = 0;
5931
5932		/* junk columns aren't visible to upper query */
5933		if (te->resjunk)
5934			continue;
5935
5936		/*
5937		 * The subquery could be an expansion of a view that's had columns
5938		 * added to it since the current query was parsed, so that there are
5939		 * non-junk tlist columns in it that don't correspond to any column
5940		 * visible at our query level.  Ignore such columns.
5941		 */
5942		if (te->resno < rel->min_attr || te->resno > rel->max_attr)
5943			continue;
5944
5945		/*
5946		 * XXX This currently doesn't work for subqueries containing set
5947		 * operations, because the Vars in their tlists are bogus references
5948		 * to the first leaf subquery, which wouldn't give the right answer
5949		 * even if we could still get to its PlannerInfo.
5950		 *
5951		 * Also, the subquery could be an appendrel for which all branches are
5952		 * known empty due to constraint exclusion, in which case
5953		 * set_append_rel_pathlist will have left the attr_widths set to zero.
5954		 *
5955		 * In either case, we just leave the width estimate zero until
5956		 * set_rel_width fixes it.
5957		 */
5958		if (IsA(texpr, Var) &&
5959			subroot->parse->setOperations == NULL)
5960		{
5961			Var		   *var = (Var *) texpr;
5962			RelOptInfo *subrel = find_base_rel(subroot, var->varno);
5963
5964			item_width = subrel->attr_widths[var->varattno - subrel->min_attr];
5965		}
5966		rel->attr_widths[te->resno - rel->min_attr] = item_width;
5967	}
5968
5969	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
5970	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
5971}
5972
5973/*
5974 * set_function_size_estimates
5975 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a function call.
5976 *
5977 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
5978 * already.
5979 *
5980 * We set the same fields as set_baserel_size_estimates.
5981 */
5982void
5983set_function_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
5984{
5985	RangeTblEntry *rte;
5986	ListCell   *lc;
5987
5988	/* Should only be applied to base relations that are functions */
5989	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
5990	rte = planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root);
5991	Assert(rte->rtekind == RTE_FUNCTION);
5992
5993	/*
5994	 * Estimate number of rows the functions will return. The rowcount of the
5995	 * node is that of the largest function result.
5996	 */
5997	rel->tuples = 0;
5998	foreach(lc, rte->functions)
5999	{
6000		RangeTblFunction *rtfunc = (RangeTblFunction *) lfirst(lc);
6001		double		ntup = expression_returns_set_rows(root, rtfunc->funcexpr);
6002
6003		if (ntup > rel->tuples)
6004			rel->tuples = ntup;
6005	}
6006
6007	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
6008	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
6009}
6010
6011/*
6012 * set_function_size_estimates
6013 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a function call.
6014 *
6015 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
6016 * already.
6017 *
6018 * We set the same fields as set_tablefunc_size_estimates.
6019 */
6020void
6021set_tablefunc_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
6022{
6023	/* Should only be applied to base relations that are functions */
6024	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
6025	Assert(planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root)->rtekind == RTE_TABLEFUNC);
6026
6027	rel->tuples = 100;
6028
6029	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
6030	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
6031}
6032
6033/*
6034 * set_values_size_estimates
6035 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a values list.
6036 *
6037 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
6038 * already.
6039 *
6040 * We set the same fields as set_baserel_size_estimates.
6041 */
6042void
6043set_values_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
6044{
6045	RangeTblEntry *rte;
6046
6047	/* Should only be applied to base relations that are values lists */
6048	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
6049	rte = planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root);
6050	Assert(rte->rtekind == RTE_VALUES);
6051
6052	/*
6053	 * Estimate number of rows the values list will return. We know this
6054	 * precisely based on the list length (well, barring set-returning
6055	 * functions in list items, but that's a refinement not catered for
6056	 * anywhere else either).
6057	 */
6058	rel->tuples = list_length(rte->values_lists);
6059
6060	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
6061	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
6062}
6063
6064/*
6065 * set_cte_size_estimates
6066 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a CTE reference.
6067 *
6068 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
6069 * already, and we need an estimate of the number of rows returned by the CTE
6070 * (if a regular CTE) or the non-recursive term (if a self-reference).
6071 *
6072 * We set the same fields as set_baserel_size_estimates.
6073 */
6074void
6075set_cte_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel, double cte_rows)
6076{
6077	RangeTblEntry *rte;
6078
6079	/* Should only be applied to base relations that are CTE references */
6080	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
6081	rte = planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root);
6082	Assert(rte->rtekind == RTE_CTE);
6083
6084	if (rte->self_reference)
6085	{
6086		/*
6087		 * In a self-reference, we assume the average worktable size is a
6088		 * multiple of the nonrecursive term's size.  The best multiplier will
6089		 * vary depending on query "fan-out", so make its value adjustable.
6090		 */
6091		rel->tuples = clamp_row_est(recursive_worktable_factor * cte_rows);
6092	}
6093	else
6094	{
6095		/* Otherwise just believe the CTE's rowcount estimate */
6096		rel->tuples = cte_rows;
6097	}
6098
6099	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
6100	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
6101}
6102
6103/*
6104 * set_namedtuplestore_size_estimates
6105 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a tuplestore reference.
6106 *
6107 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
6108 * already.
6109 *
6110 * We set the same fields as set_baserel_size_estimates.
6111 */
6112void
6113set_namedtuplestore_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
6114{
6115	RangeTblEntry *rte;
6116
6117	/* Should only be applied to base relations that are tuplestore references */
6118	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
6119	rte = planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root);
6120	Assert(rte->rtekind == RTE_NAMEDTUPLESTORE);
6121
6122	/*
6123	 * Use the estimate provided by the code which is generating the named
6124	 * tuplestore.  In some cases, the actual number might be available; in
6125	 * others the same plan will be re-used, so a "typical" value might be
6126	 * estimated and used.
6127	 */
6128	rel->tuples = rte->enrtuples;
6129	if (rel->tuples < 0)
6130		rel->tuples = 1000;
6131
6132	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
6133	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
6134}
6135
6136/*
6137 * set_result_size_estimates
6138 *		Set the size estimates for an RTE_RESULT base relation
6139 *
6140 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
6141 * already.
6142 *
6143 * We set the same fields as set_baserel_size_estimates.
6144 */
6145void
6146set_result_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
6147{
6148	/* Should only be applied to RTE_RESULT base relations */
6149	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
6150	Assert(planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root)->rtekind == RTE_RESULT);
6151
6152	/* RTE_RESULT always generates a single row, natively */
6153	rel->tuples = 1;
6154
6155	/* Now estimate number of output rows, etc */
6156	set_baserel_size_estimates(root, rel);
6157}
6158
6159/*
6160 * set_foreign_size_estimates
6161 *		Set the size estimates for a base relation that is a foreign table.
6162 *
6163 * There is not a whole lot that we can do here; the foreign-data wrapper
6164 * is responsible for producing useful estimates.  We can do a decent job
6165 * of estimating baserestrictcost, so we set that, and we also set up width
6166 * using what will be purely datatype-driven estimates from the targetlist.
6167 * There is no way to do anything sane with the rows value, so we just put
6168 * a default estimate and hope that the wrapper can improve on it.  The
6169 * wrapper's GetForeignRelSize function will be called momentarily.
6170 *
6171 * The rel's targetlist and restrictinfo list must have been constructed
6172 * already.
6173 */
6174void
6175set_foreign_size_estimates(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
6176{
6177	/* Should only be applied to base relations */
6178	Assert(rel->relid > 0);
6179
6180	rel->rows = 1000;			/* entirely bogus default estimate */
6181
6182	cost_qual_eval(&rel->baserestrictcost, rel->baserestrictinfo, root);
6183
6184	set_rel_width(root, rel);
6185}
6186
6187
6188/*
6189 * set_rel_width
6190 *		Set the estimated output width of a base relation.
6191 *
6192 * The estimated output width is the sum of the per-attribute width estimates
6193 * for the actually-referenced columns, plus any PHVs or other expressions
6194 * that have to be calculated at this relation.  This is the amount of data
6195 * we'd need to pass upwards in case of a sort, hash, etc.
6196 *
6197 * This function also sets reltarget->cost, so it's a bit misnamed now.
6198 *
6199 * NB: this works best on plain relations because it prefers to look at
6200 * real Vars.  For subqueries, set_subquery_size_estimates will already have
6201 * copied up whatever per-column estimates were made within the subquery,
6202 * and for other types of rels there isn't much we can do anyway.  We fall
6203 * back on (fairly stupid) datatype-based width estimates if we can't get
6204 * any better number.
6205 *
6206 * The per-attribute width estimates are cached for possible re-use while
6207 * building join relations or post-scan/join pathtargets.
6208 */
6209static void
6210set_rel_width(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel)
6211{
6212	Oid			reloid = planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root)->relid;
6213	int64		tuple_width = 0;
6214	bool		have_wholerow_var = false;
6215	ListCell   *lc;
6216
6217	/* Vars are assumed to have cost zero, but other exprs do not */
6218	rel->reltarget->cost.startup = 0;
6219	rel->reltarget->cost.per_tuple = 0;
6220
6221	foreach(lc, rel->reltarget->exprs)
6222	{
6223		Node	   *node = (Node *) lfirst(lc);
6224
6225		/*
6226		 * Ordinarily, a Var in a rel's targetlist must belong to that rel;
6227		 * but there are corner cases involving LATERAL references where that
6228		 * isn't so.  If the Var has the wrong varno, fall through to the
6229		 * generic case (it doesn't seem worth the trouble to be any smarter).
6230		 */
6231		if (IsA(node, Var) &&
6232			((Var *) node)->varno == rel->relid)
6233		{
6234			Var		   *var = (Var *) node;
6235			int			ndx;
6236			int32		item_width;
6237
6238			Assert(var->varattno >= rel->min_attr);
6239			Assert(var->varattno <= rel->max_attr);
6240
6241			ndx = var->varattno - rel->min_attr;
6242
6243			/*
6244			 * If it's a whole-row Var, we'll deal with it below after we have
6245			 * already cached as many attr widths as possible.
6246			 */
6247			if (var->varattno == 0)
6248			{
6249				have_wholerow_var = true;
6250				continue;
6251			}
6252
6253			/*
6254			 * The width may have been cached already (especially if it's a
6255			 * subquery), so don't duplicate effort.
6256			 */
6257			if (rel->attr_widths[ndx] > 0)
6258			{
6259				tuple_width += rel->attr_widths[ndx];
6260				continue;
6261			}
6262
6263			/* Try to get column width from statistics */
6264			if (reloid != InvalidOid && var->varattno > 0)
6265			{
6266				item_width = get_attavgwidth(reloid, var->varattno);
6267				if (item_width > 0)
6268				{
6269					rel->attr_widths[ndx] = item_width;
6270					tuple_width += item_width;
6271					continue;
6272				}
6273			}
6274
6275			/*
6276			 * Not a plain relation, or can't find statistics for it. Estimate
6277			 * using just the type info.
6278			 */
6279			item_width = get_typavgwidth(var->vartype, var->vartypmod);
6280			Assert(item_width > 0);
6281			rel->attr_widths[ndx] = item_width;
6282			tuple_width += item_width;
6283		}
6284		else if (IsA(node, PlaceHolderVar))
6285		{
6286			/*
6287			 * We will need to evaluate the PHV's contained expression while
6288			 * scanning this rel, so be sure to include it in reltarget->cost.
6289			 */
6290			PlaceHolderVar *phv = (PlaceHolderVar *) node;
6291			PlaceHolderInfo *phinfo = find_placeholder_info(root, phv);
6292			QualCost	cost;
6293
6294			tuple_width += phinfo->ph_width;
6295			cost_qual_eval_node(&cost, (Node *) phv->phexpr, root);
6296			rel->reltarget->cost.startup += cost.startup;
6297			rel->reltarget->cost.per_tuple += cost.per_tuple;
6298		}
6299		else
6300		{
6301			/*
6302			 * We could be looking at an expression pulled up from a subquery,
6303			 * or a ROW() representing a whole-row child Var, etc.  Do what we
6304			 * can using the expression type information.
6305			 */
6306			int32		item_width;
6307			QualCost	cost;
6308
6309			item_width = get_typavgwidth(exprType(node), exprTypmod(node));
6310			Assert(item_width > 0);
6311			tuple_width += item_width;
6312			/* Not entirely clear if we need to account for cost, but do so */
6313			cost_qual_eval_node(&cost, node, root);
6314			rel->reltarget->cost.startup += cost.startup;
6315			rel->reltarget->cost.per_tuple += cost.per_tuple;
6316		}
6317	}
6318
6319	/*
6320	 * If we have a whole-row reference, estimate its width as the sum of
6321	 * per-column widths plus heap tuple header overhead.
6322	 */
6323	if (have_wholerow_var)
6324	{
6325		int64		wholerow_width = MAXALIGN(SizeofHeapTupleHeader);
6326
6327		if (reloid != InvalidOid)
6328		{
6329			/* Real relation, so estimate true tuple width */
6330			wholerow_width += get_relation_data_width(reloid,
6331													  rel->attr_widths - rel->min_attr);
6332		}
6333		else
6334		{
6335			/* Do what we can with info for a phony rel */
6336			AttrNumber	i;
6337
6338			for (i = 1; i <= rel->max_attr; i++)
6339				wholerow_width += rel->attr_widths[i - rel->min_attr];
6340		}
6341
6342		rel->attr_widths[0 - rel->min_attr] = clamp_width_est(wholerow_width);
6343
6344		/*
6345		 * Include the whole-row Var as part of the output tuple.  Yes, that
6346		 * really is what happens at runtime.
6347		 */
6348		tuple_width += wholerow_width;
6349	}
6350
6351	rel->reltarget->width = clamp_width_est(tuple_width);
6352}
6353
6354/*
6355 * set_pathtarget_cost_width
6356 *		Set the estimated eval cost and output width of a PathTarget tlist.
6357 *
6358 * As a notational convenience, returns the same PathTarget pointer passed in.
6359 *
6360 * Most, though not quite all, uses of this function occur after we've run
6361 * set_rel_width() for base relations; so we can usually obtain cached width
6362 * estimates for Vars.  If we can't, fall back on datatype-based width
6363 * estimates.  Present early-planning uses of PathTargets don't need accurate
6364 * widths badly enough to justify going to the catalogs for better data.
6365 */
6366PathTarget *
6367set_pathtarget_cost_width(PlannerInfo *root, PathTarget *target)
6368{
6369	int64		tuple_width = 0;
6370	ListCell   *lc;
6371
6372	/* Vars are assumed to have cost zero, but other exprs do not */
6373	target->cost.startup = 0;
6374	target->cost.per_tuple = 0;
6375
6376	foreach(lc, target->exprs)
6377	{
6378		Node	   *node = (Node *) lfirst(lc);
6379
6380		tuple_width += get_expr_width(root, node);
6381
6382		/* For non-Vars, account for evaluation cost */
6383		if (!IsA(node, Var))
6384		{
6385			QualCost	cost;
6386
6387			cost_qual_eval_node(&cost, node, root);
6388			target->cost.startup += cost.startup;
6389			target->cost.per_tuple += cost.per_tuple;
6390		}
6391	}
6392
6393	target->width = clamp_width_est(tuple_width);
6394
6395	return target;
6396}
6397
6398/*
6399 * get_expr_width
6400 *		Estimate the width of the given expr attempting to use the width
6401 *		cached in a Var's owning RelOptInfo, else fallback on the type's
6402 *		average width when unable to or when the given Node is not a Var.
6403 */
6404static int32
6405get_expr_width(PlannerInfo *root, const Node *expr)
6406{
6407	int32		width;
6408
6409	if (IsA(expr, Var))
6410	{
6411		const Var  *var = (const Var *) expr;
6412
6413		/* We should not see any upper-level Vars here */
6414		Assert(var->varlevelsup == 0);
6415
6416		/* Try to get data from RelOptInfo cache */
6417		if (!IS_SPECIAL_VARNO(var->varno) &&
6418			var->varno < root->simple_rel_array_size)
6419		{
6420			RelOptInfo *rel = root->simple_rel_array[var->varno];
6421
6422			if (rel != NULL &&
6423				var->varattno >= rel->min_attr &&
6424				var->varattno <= rel->max_attr)
6425			{
6426				int			ndx = var->varattno - rel->min_attr;
6427
6428				if (rel->attr_widths[ndx] > 0)
6429					return rel->attr_widths[ndx];
6430			}
6431		}
6432
6433		/*
6434		 * No cached data available, so estimate using just the type info.
6435		 */
6436		width = get_typavgwidth(var->vartype, var->vartypmod);
6437		Assert(width > 0);
6438
6439		return width;
6440	}
6441
6442	width = get_typavgwidth(exprType(expr), exprTypmod(expr));
6443	Assert(width > 0);
6444	return width;
6445}
6446
6447/*
6448 * relation_byte_size
6449 *	  Estimate the storage space in bytes for a given number of tuples
6450 *	  of a given width (size in bytes).
6451 */
6452static double
6453relation_byte_size(double tuples, int width)
6454{
6455	return tuples * (MAXALIGN(width) + MAXALIGN(SizeofHeapTupleHeader));
6456}
6457
6458/*
6459 * page_size
6460 *	  Returns an estimate of the number of pages covered by a given
6461 *	  number of tuples of a given width (size in bytes).
6462 */
6463static double
6464page_size(double tuples, int width)
6465{
6466	return ceil(relation_byte_size(tuples, width) / BLCKSZ);
6467}
6468
6469/*
6470 * Estimate the fraction of the work that each worker will do given the
6471 * number of workers budgeted for the path.
6472 */
6473static double
6474get_parallel_divisor(Path *path)
6475{
6476	double		parallel_divisor = path->parallel_workers;
6477
6478	/*
6479	 * Early experience with parallel query suggests that when there is only
